text: The surgeon dissects the mesosigmoid during phase one of the procedure, ligating and dividing the inferior mesenteric pedicle.
The surgeon performs pelvic dissection, dividing the rectum using laparoscopic techniques.

The patient is positioned in the Trendelenburg position with the right side down. The surgeon and cameraman are positioned on the right side of the patient, while the first assistant stands on the left. Monitors are visible at the foot of the bed.
The surgeon operates through the right-sided cannulas, utilizing the laparoscopic setup to perform the procedure.
The surgeon mobilizes the left colon and splenic flexure during phase two of the procedure, using laparoscopic instruments through the previously placed cannulas.
The surgeon operates through the suprapubic and left lower quadrant cannulas, standing between the patient's legs, while the assistant and cameraman are positioned on the patient's right side.
The surgical team is focused on the monitor positioned near the patient's left shoulder, observing the ongoing procedure.
The surgeon mobilizes the right colon and hepatic flexure, using surgical instruments to carefully separate these structures from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon, positioned between the patient's legs, operates through the suprapubic and right lower quadrant cannulas, manipulating the surgical instruments within the abdominal cavity.
The assistant and cameraman reposition themselves to the patient's left side, altering their stance in the operating room.
The assistant grasps the mesosigmoid and retracts it anteriorly, while the surgeon incises the peritoneum to the right of the inferior mesenteric artery.
The surgeon applies continuous traction to the peritoneum, dissecting it down to the sacral promontory and then upwards to the level of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA).
The surgeon uses blunt dissection to sweep the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) anteriorly, carefully separating it from the preaortic hypogastric neural plexus.
The surgeon continues medial dissection beneath the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) and superior rectal artery, identifying the left ureter and gonadal vessels, which are then swept posteriorly.
The inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) is visible on the left side of the surgical field, with the ureter discernible in the background.
The surgeon creates a peritoneal window just lateral to the inferior mesenteric artery and vein, revealing the underlying structures.
The surgeon drapes the vascular pedicle over the grasper, maintaining control of the structure during the procedure.

The surgeon uses an endoscopic vascular stapler to ligate the inferior mesenteric artery and vein below the left colic artery, ensuring the left ureter is clearly identified and retracted to avoid injury.
The surgeon highlights the left ureter, which is visible in the surgical field, ensuring it is clearly identified and protected during the procedure.
Following the ligation of the vascular pedicle, the surgeon continues dissection in the retroperitoneum, elevating the mesosigmoid towards the lateral abdominal wall.
The surgeon mobilizes the sigmoid colon, moving it downward past the sacral promontory, with the surrounding tissues visibly retracted.
The surgeon dissects and takes down the lateral peritoneal attachments, further mobilizing the sigmoid colon.
The surgeon observes the thin tissue layers behind the mesosigmoid, which have been developed during the dissection process.
The sigmoid colon is fully mobilized, with the surrounding tissue and structures clearly visible after the completion of dissection and elevation of the mesosigmoid.
A member of the operating team uses a rigid proctoscope to identify the distal resection line on the rectum.
The surgeon begins dissection of the mesorectum on the right side, focusing on separating the mesorectal tissue from surrounding structures.
The surgeon manages larger vessels by applying clips or using an endoscopic linear stapler, ensuring secure closure of the vessels during the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon clears the distal resection line on the rectum, which has been highlighted by a member of the operating team.
The surgeon divides the rectum using a three-centimeter endoscopic stapler, firing the device twice to complete the division.
Following the division of the rectum, the surgical team initiates phase two of the colectomy procedure.

The assistant passes an endoscopic snare through the right upper quadrant cannula. Using a grasper, the cut end of the colon is pulled through the loop, and the colon distal to the loop is flipped over the shaft of the snare retractor to expose the posterior mesenteric attachments.
The surgeon continues the dissection in a cephalad direction, progressing upwards along the colon to further separate tissue attachments.
The surgeon sharply divides the lateral peritoneal attachments in the left colonic mesentery, while Girota's fascia is swept away from the posterior surface of the colonic mesentery.
The surgeon divides the base of the left mesocolon and takes down the lateral peritoneal attachments, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The snare retractor is steadily moved up the colon to obtain maximal traction on the peritoneal attachments, enhancing exposure of the posterior mesenteric attachments.
The surgeon takes down the splenic flexure by dividing the peritoneal attachments close to the bowel, while applying traction with the snare retractor to facilitate exposure.
The bowel is retracted to the right, revealing the proper surgical plane for further dissection.
The greater omentum is gradually exposed and separated from the colon using sharp dissection in the avascular plane adjacent to the bowel wall.
The surgeon removes the omentum from the colon, progressing from the left side to the right side, revealing the underlying bowel structure.
The surgeon periodically moves the loop proximally to enhance traction on the bowel, facilitating the dissection process.
The surgeon removes the colon from the snare retractor and places it into the left abdomen after dissecting as far to the right as possible.

The assistant grasps the dorsal mesenteric attachments of the terminal ileum and retracts them anteriorly. The surgeon incises the retroperitoneal attachments of the terminal ileum, located just medial to the cecum, and continues the dissection cephalad and to the left toward the inferior edge of the duodenum.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the ileal and right colonic mesentery from the retroperitoneum by creating a tunnel over the duodenum using blunt dissection.
The surgeon identifies the ileocolic artery and the right colic artery on the dorsal surface of the mesentery, preparing for further vascular dissection.
The surgeon creates windows on the ventral surface of the mesentery, carefully dissecting around the vessels to separate them from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon highlights the ileocolic artery, emphasizing its location and visibility within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses an endoscopic vascular stapler to ligate and divide the ileocolic pedicle, completing the separation of this vascular structure from the surrounding tissue.

The surgeon proceeds with dissection up the mesentery towards the middle colic vessels after dividing the ileocolic artery, noting that the right colic artery typically does not originate from the superior mesenteric artery.
The surgeon highlights the duodenum and pancreas, visually distinguishing these structures within the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies and clears the middle colic vessels, then ligates them using either large clips or an endoscopic linear stapler.
The surgeon divides the remaining transverse mesocolon after controlling the middle colic vessels, using surgical instruments to separate the mesocolon tissue.
The surgeon visualizes the lesser sac through the divided transverse mesocolon, providing a clear view of the anatomical space.
The surgeon detaches the omentum from the proximal transverse colon, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The transverse colon is positioned downward, and the surgeon divides the remaining omental attachments.
The surgeon detaches the right colon and hepatic flexure from the retroperitoneum, beginning at the cecum, completing the separation of these structures.
The surgeon addresses the remaining minor lateral and posterior attachments of the colon, following extensive dorsal mobilization.
The surgeon has lowered the hepatic flexure and is moving the colon into the pelvis in preparation for extraction.
The surgeon converts the suprapubic cannula site into a small phantastial incision, through which the specimen is extracted from the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon divides the ileum and places an O polypropylene suture around the cut edge of the ileum, securing the divided section.
The surgeon places the anvil and center rod of the circular stapler into the ileum and secures them with a purse string suture, preparing for anastomosis.
The surgeon returns the ileum to the abdominal cavity and irrigates the abdomen, ensuring the surgical field is clean.
The surgeon closes the phan and steel incision, then reestablishes pneumoperitoneum in the abdominal cavity.
Under laparoscopic guidance, the surgeon performs a standard double stapling technique to create the ileo-rectal anastomosis, visually aligning and securing the ileum to the rectum.
The surgeon carefully examines the cut edge of the ileal mesentery to ensure it is not twisted before proceeding with anastomotic formation.
The surgeon fills the pelvis with irrigation fluid and occludes the bowel just proximal to the anastomosis. Using a proctoscope, air is insufflated into the rectum to check the anastomosis for leaks.
